After retiring from football, Dave Duerson briefly owned a McDonald’s franchise in Louisville, Kentucky, before purchasing a controlling interest in Fair Oaks Farms (formerly Brooks Sausage Company) in 1995. Under his leadership, sales grew significantly before he sold his stake in 2002. He later founded Duerson Foods, which went into receivership in 2006, with its assets auctioned off.

Dorothy Paget's mother was a member of the New York Whitney family, which was one of the most prominent thoroughbred horse racing and breeding families in America. Paget too owned a stable of thoroughbreds as well as the Ballymacoll Stud breeding farm in County Meath, Ireland. Her horses won a total of 1,532 races in both flat and hurdling. She was the British flat racing Champion Owner in 1943, the year her horse Straight Deal won The Derby. She was leading National Hunt owner in 1933–34, 1940–41 and 1951–52. She owned seven Cheltenham Gold Cup winners, Golden Miller five times, 1932–1936 inclusive, Roman Hackle in 1940 and Mont Tremblant in 1952. Her four Champion Hurdle winners were Insurance in 1932 and 1933, Solford in 1940 and Distel in 1946. Golden Miller also provided her with her solitary victory in the Grand National in 1934, still the only occasion any horse has won the two major prizes of British steeplechasing in the same season.

Runic inscription U 1050 is the Rundata listing for an inscription on a granite stone that is 1 meter in height. The inscription consists of runic text carved on an intertwined serpent. The inscription is classified as being carved in runestone style Pr 4, which is also known as Urnes style. Although unsigned, the inscription is considered to have been carved by the same runemaster that made inscriptions U 1048 at this same church and U 1060 in Tibble.

The second season of *Super Singer Junior* premiered on 21 June 2009 and was telecast on Vijay TV from Monday to Thursday at 9:00 pm. Participants gained recognition from renowned figures in the music industry, with the winner initially promised a cash prize of Rs. 25 lakhs. During the top 25 performance rounds, the prize was revised to Rs. 25 lakhs in the form of a home sponsored by Arun Excello. Bharti Airtel returned as the title sponsor for the season, which aimed to discover Tamil Nadu’s sweetest voice.

In 1995 Manfred Feist suffered a stroke, after which he was paralysed down one side of his body. Following a long period of ill-health he died in Berlin on 17 December 2012. His death was made public only after a delay of more than two weeks, when an announcement was placed in the Berliner Zeitung. Press speculation focused on the question of whether Margot Honecker would return to Germany from her Chilean exile for her younger brother's funeral.

Hoplites also carried a sword, mostly a short sword called a xiphos, but later also longer and heavier types. The short sword was a secondary weapon, used if or when their spears were broken or lost, or if the phalanx broke rank. The xiphos usually had a blade around 60 centimetres (24 in) long; however, those used by the Spartans were often only 30–45 centimetres long. This very short xiphos would be very advantageous in the press that occurred when two lines of hoplites met, capable of being thrust through gaps in the shieldwall into an enemy's unprotected groin or throat, while there was no room to swing a longer sword. Such a small weapon would be particularly useful after many hoplites had started to abandon body armour during the Peloponnesian War. Hoplites could also alternatively carry the kopis, a heavy knife with a forward-curving blade. The scabbard of the sword was called koleos (κολεός).

Using single-cell mRNA sequencing, sensory-modality specific primary afferent have been molecularly defined into clusters based on gene expression patterns. Here, 11 sub clusters were detected; NF1-3, transmitting innocuous nociceptive information; NF4-5, which transmit proprioceptive information; NP1-3, transmitting itch information; PEP1-2, nociceptive information and TH, which is involved in pleasant touch, The pruriceptive NP1-3 were shown to express genes related to histaminergic and non-histaminergic signaling, where NF1 expresses genes responding to lysophosphatidic acid (Lpar3 and Lpar5), NP2 chloroquine-responsive genes (Mrgpra3 and Mrgprx1), whereas NP3 expresses neuropeptides Nppb and Sst as well as genes involved in inflammatory itch (Il31ra, Osmr and Crystrl2). The histamine receptor gene Hrh1 was found in NP2 and NP3, suggesting that histaminergic itch is transmitted by both these pruriceptive sub clusters.

Diagnosed with HIV in 1985, Davis kept his condition private until shortly before his death at age 41 on September 8, 1991, in Los Angeles. It was revealed in a book proposal that Davis had written before his death that he had to keep his medical condition a secret to be able to continue to work and support his family. He is interred at Forest Lawn Memorial Park in the Hollywood Hills. In 1997 his wife Susan revealed that he committed assisted suicide by a drug overdose.

Henry Letheby (1816–1876) was born in Plymouth in 1816. He began studying chemistry at the Cornwall Polytechnic Society in Falmouth and commenced medical studies in 1837 at Aldersgate Street medical school, where he became an assistant to Jonathan Pereira. Letheby graduated M.B. from the University of London in 1842.

At the end of the season, Connaboy was among a number of players granted two weeks by the SFA to agree terms with their club, failing which they would be transfer-listed. Nine days later, he signed for Division One club Cowdenbeath. Over the first few months of the season, he occupied both half-back positions, and scored his first goal of the season in December from the inside-left position, in a 3–2 defeat of Falkirk in which Cowdenbeath's goalkeeper injured a shoulder and came back after treatment to partner Connaboy on the left wing and set up the winning goal. He was also one of several tried without success at centre forward. In March, Cowdenbeath fined Connaboy £3 for refusing to report on a Sunday to have an injury assessed; he took the matter to the SFA to see if the club had the right to require his attendance on a Sunday (which was a common occurrence, particularly for treatment of injuries), but his appeal was unsuccessful.

Pinipig, made from immature glutinous rice, are light green when fresh but turn yellowish white to brown when toasted. They resemble oats, are often confused with puffed rice, and have a crunchy exterior with a chewy center.

Charles Cathcart, 8th Lord Cathcart was born in about 1686 and had a distinguished military career. He became a major on the Scots Grays in 1709. During the Jacobite rising of 1715 the eighth Lord Cathcart commanded a detachment of dragoons and it was Cathcart's troops that outflanked the Jacobite forces at Battle of Sheriffmuir. Cathcart had also previously defeated the Jacobites at the Skirmish of Dunfermline in October 1715.

Bruni was educated at The Loomis Chaffee School, an independent boarding and day college preparatory school in Windsor, Connecticut, followed by the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, from which he graduated Phi Beta Kappa in 1986 with a B.A. in English. He was a Morehead Scholar and wrote for the student paper, The Daily Tar Heel. He then attended Columbia University's Graduate School of Journalism, from which he graduated second in his class with a master of science degree in journalism, and also won a Pulitzer Traveling Fellowship.

The 18th edition, in 2002, exceptionally awarded 13 people as "Master of National Comics" instead of the traditional three names. This would be a tribute to the "majority" of the event (it was "turning 18 years old"). It was the largest number of winners in this category in the same edition.

The first episode of the season premiered on 15 July 2009, and episodes were telecast on Vijay TV between Monday to Thursday. Children from the age of 6 years to the age of 14 years were permitted to audition to showcase their talent on Vijay TV's platform. Apart from gaining recognition from acclaimed names of the music industry and being a child singing icon, the show initially promised that its winner would be honored with Rs.25 lakhs in cash prize money. Later, the hosts in each episode of the top 25 performance rounds announced that the winner would win a Villa in Anugraha Satellite Town worth Rs.25 lakhs from its season 2 sponsor, Navashakthi Township and Developers.

The Edgewater Beach Apartments, also known as the Edgewater Beach Co-op, were built in 1928 at the north end of the Edgewater Beach Hotel property and are the only surviving part of the historic complex. Designed by renowned architect Benjamin Marshall, who also created the Edgewater Beach Hotel and several elegant co-ops along Lake Shore Drive, the 19-story "sunset pink" building complements the former hotel's "sunrise yellow" exterior. Located near Lake Shore Drive, the apartments are a Chicago Landmark and part of the Bryn Mawr Historic District, known for its Art Deco architecture. The building’s distinctive pink hue makes it unique as Chicago’s only pink high-rise. In 1994, the Edgewater Beach Apartments were added to the National Register of Historic Places, cementing their status as an architectural and cultural icon.

*Kyon Ki* was released on November 2, 2005, coinciding with Diwali in India. The romantic drama performed poorly, grossing Rs 22.8 crore worldwide. On the same day, Priyadarshan's comedy *Garam Masala* released, earning Rs 53.57 crore globally and emerging as a box office hit.

Bachenbülach has an area of 4.3 km2 (1.7 sq mi). Of this area, 35.1% is used for agricultural purposes, while 41.2% is forested. Of the rest of the land, 20.8% is settled (buildings or roads) and the remainder (2.8%) is non-productive (rivers, glaciers or mountains).

The Cleveland Cavaliers debuted new uniforms before the start of the 2010-11 NBA season, marking a return to the original shades of wine and gold used from 1970 to 1983. The home uniform is white, featuring a wine-and-gold horizontal stripe trim on the collar, sleeves, waistband, and pant legs, with the word "CAVALIERS" displayed in block-style wine lettering across the front. The road uniform is wine-colored with gold lettering for the player's name and navy blue numerals on both the front and back, with "Cleveland" prominently featured across the chest. An alternate jersey was introduced during this period, further expanding the team's refreshed look. Additionally, the team motto, "All for One. One for All," is stitched on the inside of the collar of the uniforms, symbolizing unity and pride in the Cavaliers' identity.

On December 12, 2011, Carrier IQ released a 19-page document titled "Understanding Carrier IQ Technology," credited to Trevor Eckhart, Ashkan Soltani, Dan Rosenberg, and Carrier IQ itself. The document provides a detailed breakdown of how the software operates on mobile phones, explaining that carrier-provided profiles determine what data is collected. It states that carriers receive metrics such as location, call activity, and data usage. The document also notes that future updates may refine the software's functionality and data collection practices, emphasizing transparency and regulatory compliance.

On December 12, 2011, Carrier IQ released a document to explain their software and its uses. The document is titled "Understanding Carrier IQ Technology". There are credits given to Dan Rosenberg and Trevor Eckhart. The nineteen-page document provides a technical breakdown of how the software on a mobile phone works with "profiles" provided by the carrier to give the carriers performance data of their networks and devices running on them. The document appears as if it will be updated on a regular basis as more questions are answered over time.

The National High Performance Computing Center (NHPCC) at Istanbul Technical University supports advanced research and computational needs. Its supercomputer ranked in the Top500 list, achieving global recognition.

Delayed-choice experiments raise questions about time and time sequences, and thereby bring the usual ideas of time and causal sequence into question. If events at D1, D2, D3, D4 determine outcomes at D0, then effect seems to precede cause. If the idler light paths were greatly extended so that a year goes by before a photon shows up at D1, D2, D3, or D4, then when a photon shows up in one of these detectors, it would cause a signal photon to have shown up in a certain mode a year earlier. Alternatively, knowledge of the future fate of the idler photon would determine the activity of the signal photon in its own present. Neither of these ideas conforms to the usual human expectation of causality. However, knowledge of the future, which would be a hidden variable, was refuted in experiments.

"Give It Up," Bonnie Raitt's second studio album, was recorded in 1972 at Bearsville Studios in Woodstock, New York, and produced by Willie Murphy. The album blends folk, blues, and rock, showcasing Raitt's versatility as a musician. Featuring ten tracks, it includes a mix of originals and covers, such as songs by Sippie Wallace and Jackson Browne. The faster tracks incorporate New Orleans-style brass instrumentation, with the opening track, "Give It Up or Let Me Go," beginning with a rollicking Dixieland brass intro before transitioning into a bluesy groove. Guest musicians, many from the Chicago blues scene, include Junior Wells on harmonica and Buddy Guy. Compared to her raw, unpolished debut, "Give It Up" offers a more refined sound while retaining the earthy, soulful essence of her earlier work. The album's standout brass arrangements, particularly on "Give It Up or Let Me Go," highlight its New Orleans influence, adding a lively, celebratory energy to the record.

The closing ceremony began on November 27, 2010, at 20:06 local time in front of 35,000 spectators. The show had the theme "Leave Your Song Here", which had cultural displays from China, India, Indonesia, Lebanon, Japan, Kazakhstan and Mongolia. The ceremony featured songs from different cultures as chosen: the Indians "Saajan ji Ghar Aaye" and "Aao re Jhumo re", Indonesian "Sing Sing So" and Japanese "Sakura". Various artists from Taiwan, Hong Kong and mainland China performed "Triumphant Return", among them were Alan Tam, Leo Ku and Hacken Lee.

Tracy Reiner, born Tracy Henry on July 7, 1964, in Albuquerque, New Mexico, is the daughter of actress and director Penny Marshall and Michael Henry, a former college football player. She is the niece of filmmaker Garry Marshall. After her mother married director Rob Reiner, he adopted Tracy and raised her as his own. Her mother was of Italian, English, German, and Scottish descent. Tracy’s adoptive paternal grandparents were actor and comedian Carl Reiner and actress Estelle Reiner. Notably, 15 of her relatives worked at labs run by the United States Atomic Energy Commission, reflecting a unique family history beyond entertainment.

Edmund Lander leased the area known as the Mooloolah Back Plain from the Queensland Government in 1861. He, along with other pioneers, harvested the timber along the Blackall Range. When the timber was harvested, the bark was stripped, making them slippery from the sap and a sharp point was made on one end of the tree. The tree was then pushed over the eastern side of the cliff down "chutes" (to be collected at the bottom, and transported to the nearest mills). Landers Shoot (a corruption of Lander's Chute) was the base region where the logs arrived at the base of the cliff.

The Star (stylised as THE STAR) is a brand operated by First Portsmouth, Fareham & Gosport, running along the A3 corridor. It replaced the Portsdown and Horndean tram service, which had been superseded by motor buses in 1935. Launched in 2008 with the creation of the A3 corridor, the brand encompasses two routes. Route 7 runs along London Road (A2407) from Portsmouth University via Cosham and Crookhorn, ending at Wecock Farm. Route 8 starts at Old Portsmouth Clarence Pier, passing through The Hard, Cosham, Purbrook, and Horndean before terminating at Catherington. Together, these routes provide frequent service along this busy corridor.

The name "Luster" originates from "Lustr," which initially referred to the fjord now called Lustrafjorden, a branch of Sognefjord. Derived from *ljóss*, meaning "light" or "bright," it likely described the fjord's shimmering waters. Before 1918, the name was written as "Lyster."

In November 2015, Dion's official website announced first shows of her upcoming Summer Tour 2016. Dion toured Belgium, France and Canada between 20 June 2016 and 31 August 2016, and performed a total of twenty-eight shows. The sold-out tour garnered positive reviews, with Dion performing several songs never performed live before, mainly from the 1 fille & 4 types album. The setlist featured only few songs from the upcoming Encore un soir album: "Trois heures vingt", "Encore un soir", "Ordinaire" and "À la plus haute branche" (added in Canada). On 7 September 2016, Dion performed "Encore un soir" and "Les yeux au ciel" for the very first time on television on M6's Music Show – 100% tubes 2016 in France. On 1 October 2016, she also performed "Encore un soir" and "Les yeux au ciel" on Le Grand Show on France 2. Both shows were recorded in June 2016, while Dion was touring France. In the summer of 2017, Dion toured Europe with her Celine Dion Live 2017 concert tour.

Burketown is located on the Albert River 2,115 kilometres (1,314 mi) to the north west of the state capital, Brisbane, with the nearest larger town being Normanton, 227 kilometres (141 mi) to the east, and the nearest city being Mount Isa, 425 kilometres (264 mi) to the south. The town is roughly 30 kilometres (19 mi) inland from the Gulf of Carpentaria. It is located 898 kilometres (558 mi) west of Cairns via the Savannah Way passing through the area known as the Gulf Savannah.

Frank Bruni attended The Loomis Chaffee School, a co-educational boarding school in Windsor, Connecticut, before earning a B.A. in English from the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill in 1986 as a Phi Beta Kappa graduate and Morehead Scholar. While at UNC, he wrote for *The Daily Tar Heel*. He later earned an M.S. in journalism from Columbia University's Graduate School of Journalism in 1988, graduating second in his class with highest honors and receiving a Pulitzer Traveling Fellowship.

The establishment of the Congo Pedicle granted Katanga access to the mineral-rich Copperbelt, a region divided between the Democratic Republic of Congo (DR Congo) and Northern Rhodesia (now Zambia) by the Luapula River, which served as the geographical boundary. This division left the Congolese Copperbelt under Katanga's control, while Northern Rhodesia retained its portion to the south. The British South Africa Company (BSAC) failed to secure the Garanganza kingdom as a British protectorate due to the assassination of Msiri by Omer Bodson, the Belgian second-in-command of the Congo Free State (CFS) expedition led by Canadian Captain William Grant Stairs. Stairs, viewed by some in Northern Rhodesia as a mercenary and traitor, ensured Katanga's annexation to the CFS. British missionaries, including those of the Plymouth Brethren, maintained strict neutrality in political matters, avoiding involvement in the events that led to Msiri's death and the collapse of his empire.

Brache married Dolores Grecia Bernard González, with whom he had nine children. In 1947, one of his sons, Rafael Jr., was involved in the abortive Cayo Confites expedition, in which Dominican and Cuban insurrectionaries (including a young Fidel Castro) sought to overthrow Trujillo by force. Most of Brache's children remained in the United States, and a grandson, Tom Perez, became Secretary of Labor in the Obama Administration and in 2017, he became the Democratic National Committee Chair. Perez has frequently mentioned his grandfather in political speeches, including in his Senate confirmation hearing. A July 2016 piece in The Wall Street Journal suggested that he had downplayed or misrepresented Brache's connections with Trujillo. In response, Perez implied that the article had been produced as an attempt to smear him while he was under consideration for the Democratic Party's vice-president nomination.

Amin was furious upon learning of the raid, and reportedly boasted that he could have taught the Israelis a lesson if he had known that they would strike. Following the raid, Maliyamungu had 14 soldiers arrested under suspicion of collaborating with the Israelis. Once they were gathered in a room at Makindye Barracks, he shot 12 of them with his pistol. Uganda Army Chief of Staff Mustafa Adrisi reportedly wanted to incarcerate or execute Godwin Sule, the Entebbe Air Base commander, who was absent from his post during the raid. Sule had left the air base early that day to meet a female companion at Lake Victoria Hotel on 4 July. Despite Adrisi's demands, Sule's closeness to President Amin guaranteed his safety.

In schizophrenia, impairments in theory of mind (ToM) are linked to disrupted activity in brain regions such as the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) and the right temporo-parietal junction (TPJ). Reduced mPFC activity is associated with social dysfunction, including difficulties in understanding others' intentions and beliefs. This dysfunction may stem from an imbalance in excitatory and inhibitory neural activity in the mPFC, which can be exacerbated by social stress. Increased mPFC activity, on the other hand, supports evidence accumulation and memory reactivation, which are crucial for social cognition. Disruptions in these ToM-related regions may heighten social impairments and contribute to persistent hallucinations in schizophrenia.

According to the 2000 census, Cuarny had 25,992 residents (80.2% of the population) who were Roman Catholic and 2,255 individuals (7.0%) who belonged to the Swiss Reformed Church. There were 464 members (1.43%) of an Orthodox church and 497 individuals (1.53%) who belonged to another Christian church. The census recorded 5 Jewish individuals and 985 (3.04%) who were Islamic. Additionally, 1,212 residents (3.74%) belonged to no church, were agnostic, or atheist, while 1,109 individuals (3.42%) did not answer the question about religious affiliation.

By 2009, Ed Butowsky was managing investments for a hundred professional athletes. He co-founded the Clubhouse Investment Club with professional baseball player Torii Hunter, aiming to educate athletes on financial strategies. Previously, Butowsky served as a senior vice president at Bear Stearns, leveraging his expertise to guide high-net-worth clients and athletes in wealth management.

In Operation Desert Storm of 1991, 249 USAF F-16s flew 13,340 sorties in strikes against Iraq, the most of any Coalition aircraft. Falcons often had AGM-65s (up to six), or two Mk84 2,000 lb bombs (middle underwing pylons), two 1,400 L fuel tanks, two AIM-9 and an underbelly ECM pod, such as the AN/ALQ-131. F-16Ds from the 388th Tactical Fighter Wing at Hill AFB were used as spotter aircraft to search and find Iraqi SAMs and Republican Guard troops. They were armed with up to six Mk82s, Cluster Bombs, and LGBs. These aircraft were also equipped with LANTIRN and binoculars. Three aircraft were lost to confirmed enemy action: two to enemy radar guided SA-6 and SA-3 surface-to-air missiles (SAMs) and one to a shoulder-launched SA-16 missile. Other F-16s were damaged in accidents and by hostile ground fire but were able to return to base and be repaired. In all, seven F-16 were lost during Desert Storm combat operations between January 16 and February 28.

The village of Barak, Kyrgyzstan's only exclave in the Fergana Valley, has a population of approximately 627. Surrounded by Uzbek territory, it lies on the road from Osh to Khodjaabad, about 4 kilometers northwest of the Kyrgyz–Uzbek border. Administratively, Barak is part of the Kara-Suu District in Kyrgyzstan's Osh Region.

Owen Thomas Jones was born on April 16, 1878, in Blaenffynon, Newcastle-Emlyn, Carmarthenshire, to David Jones. He attended Pencader Grammar School before studying Natural Sciences at University College, Aberystwyth, graduating in 1900. That same year, he matriculated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he pursued geology. Jones was awarded a B.A. in Natural Sciences in 1902, achieving first-class honors in both parts of the Natural Sciences Tripos.

Surendra Nath Mitra was probably born in 1850. His early life was carefree and Bohemian, marked by promiscuity and drinking, with an initial indifference to religion that left him deeply unsettled and despondent.

The first written record of cannabis in South Africa was made by Jan van Riebeeck, who ordered officers of the Voorman to purchase "daccha" in Natal for trade with the Khoikhoi. The Dutch East India Company sought to monopolize cannabis sales and prohibited Cape settlers from cultivating it in 1680. However, the widespread availability of cannabis in the wild and through indigenous trade undermined profits. Consequently, the prohibition was lifted in 1700, as efforts to control the market proved ineffective.

Pinipig are characteristically light green in color when fresh, but usually become yellowish white to brown when toasted. They superficially resemble grains of oats, and are often confused with puffed rice. The texture is crunchy on the exterior with a chewy center.

The 2016 Ghostbusters reboot confirms through its marketing that a version of Egon Spengler, originally portrayed by Harold Ramis, exists in its universe. A tie-in video reveals that Dr. Jillian Holtzmann, played by Kate McKinnon, and Dr. Egon Spengler collaborated to create the film's proton packs, linking the reboot to the original series.

From 1976 to 1980, Janet Mills served as an assistant attorney general in Maine, prosecuting crimes such as murder, tax evasion, and fraud. In 1980, she was elected district attorney for Androscoggin, Franklin, and Oxford counties, becoming the first woman district attorney in New England. She was reelected three times. In 1994, Mills ran in the Democratic primary for Maine's 2nd Congressional District, seeking to replace then-Representative Olympia Snowe, but she lost to John Baldacci in the primary.

Sea buckthorn has been used over centuries in traditional medicine. Although sea buckthorn fruit extracts are under preliminary research for their pharmacological effects, there is no high-quality clinical evidence for the ability of Hippophae products to lower the risk of human diseases, and as of 2020 none are approved as prescription drugs by any national regulatory agency. Berry oil from seeds or fruit pulp, either taken orally as a dietary supplement or applied topically, is believed to be a skin softener or medicine, but there is inadequate clinical evidence of its effectiveness. There have been no systematic studies of toxicity and safety for any Hippophae product.

JoAnn Berman began her costume design career during the advent of punk rock, working at the iconic Club 57 in New York City, a hub for avant-garde artists like Keith Haring and Jean-Michel Basquiat. While at Club 57, she created costumes for the musical *The Sound of Muzak* and collaborated with Cookie Mueller at the legendary FOOD Restaurant. Following this period, Berman gained recognition for her upcycled clothing, including one-of-a-kind bustiers sold at Patricia Field’s boutique. In 1983, she met Al Diaz, Basquiat’s former SAMO(c) collaborator, at a downtown art event. The two became common-law partners for five years, during which they traveled to Puerto Rico in 1988. Their relationship was marked by challenges, including Diaz’s struggle with substance abuse, stemming from a violent assault in 1987. Despite these difficulties, Berman’s innovative designs and creative resilience solidified her reputation as a pioneer in upcycled fashion and a key figure in New York’s punk and art scenes.

From the 2000 census, 131 or 21.2% were Roman Catholic, while 316 or 51.1% belonged to the Swiss Reformed Church. Of the rest of the population, there were 2 members of an Orthodox church (or about 0.32% of the population), and there were 6 individuals (or about 0.97% of the population) who belonged to another Christian church. There were 29 (or about 4.69% of the population) who were Islamic. 109 (or about 17.64% of the population) belonged to no church, are agnostic or atheist, and 27 individuals (or about 4.37% of the population) did not answer the question.

Townsend joined the Tottenham Hotspur academy at the age of eight. In March 2009, he went on loan to Yeovil Town, making his football league debut against Milton Keynes Dons alongside fellow Spurs academy player Jonathan Obika. He made ten appearances and scored one goal for Yeovil as they avoided relegation.

In 2016, Jesper Svensson qualified for and won the PBA Tournament of Champions, held at FireLake Arena in Shawnee, Oklahoma, on February 7. At 20 years old, he became the youngest winner of the event, breaking Marshall Holman’s 1976 record. Svensson also claimed the Brunswick Euro Challenge in Munich, Germany, and the World Bowling Tour Thailand event in Bangkok, bringing his total PBA titles to five by year’s end. His victories solidified his status as a rising star in professional bowling, with his Tournament of Champions win marking a historic milestone.

Barry Stanton was born in London on 23 January 1941. The Stanton family—Stella Rose, Carl Leopold, Barry John and Rodney—migrated to Australia in 1948. They settled in the Sydney suburb of Neutral Bay. At the age of 15 Stanton became interested in rock and roll. He left school and worked as apprentice motor mechanic. His early bands included the Boppers and the Bellairs; they played gigs at local dance halls. Stanton provided "some Presley-type singing and guitar-playing." In 1957 he recorded material, which was not released until 1988, issued on his compilation album, A Tribute to the King Rare Songs 1957-1965, via Canetoad Records. According to Ainslie Baker of The Australian Women's Weekly, he "has brown eyes, is 6ft. 1in. tall, weighs 13-1⁄2 stone, likes cars and car-racing, surfing, and football."

Mosquito-borne diseases, such as dengue fever and malaria, typically affect developing countries and areas with tropical climates. Mosquito vectors are sensitive to climate changes and tend to follow seasonal patterns. Between years there are often dramatic shifts in incidence rates. The occurrence of this phenomenon in endemic areas makes mosquito-borne viruses difficult to treat.

After her nomination was withdrawn, Kathleen McCree Lewis continued working at Dykema Gossett. In 2006, Michigan Lawyers Weekly named her one of Michigan's top 10 lawyers. In an interview with the magazine, Lewis reflected on her commitment to community service, describing it as part of her essence. She credited her parents, who were very active in the community during her upbringing, for instilling the belief that responsible grownups serve their communities in a voluntary capacity. "It never occurred to me not to serve," she explained, emphasizing her dedication to civic responsibility.

Barton Rubenstein grew up in an artistic and nature-filled environment, shaped by his mother, Daryl Reich Rubenstein, a Smithsonian curator and art historian, and his father, Lee Rubenstein, who led the family in crafting large-scale snow sculptures. He attended Sidwell Friends School in Washington, D.C., and spent much of his childhood creating projects and exploring the outdoors through sailing and canoeing in Maine and Canada. These experiences, along with frequent visits to museums, deeply influenced his artistic vision, particularly his fascination with water and wind, which later became central themes in his sculptural work.
Cary Ann Hearst, whose parents are from Charleston County, South Carolina, is married to Michael Trent, her musical partner in Shovels & Rope.

Vibhuti (☰) is the holy ash obtained from sacred puja rites involving fire. Also a variant called Basma used as Vibhuti is prepared from the purified ashes of cow dung. Ash is considered a sign of purity due to its powdery white color. It is used on the forehead, normally as three horizontal lines representing Shiva. Some Hindus meld both the three horizontal vibhuti lines of Shiva and the 'U' shape thilaka of Vishnu in an amalgam marker signifying Hari-Hara (Vishnu-Shiva). In addition, sacred ash signifies that the body's origin is from dust and ash and to dust and ash it shall return. The ash is a marker of impermanence. Everything in the interim is but an illusion (maya).

The National ABU Robocon 2017 was held at Shri Shiv Chhatrapati Krida Sankul, Pune, from 2-4 March 2017. The theme was "Asobi: The Landing Disc." The College of Engineering, Pune, emerged as the winner, while the Maharashtra Institute of Technology, Pune, secured the position of first runner-up.

Isra Hirsi, who grew up in Minneapolis, Minnesota, is the daughter of U.S. Congresswoman Ilhan Omar. At age 12, she participated in a protest at the Mall of America demanding justice for Jamar Clark. She attended Minneapolis South High School, graduating in 2021. Hirsi became involved in climate activism during her freshman year of high school by joining her school’s environmental club, which sparked her passion for environmental justice.

The Paul Bunyan Axe, created by Wisconsin's National W Club, was instituted as the trophy for the Minnesota–Wisconsin football rivalry in 1948. Its 6-foot-long handle records the scores of each game. A new axe was created in 2000, and the original was donated to the College Football Hall of Fame in 2003.

El Rio was launched as hull number 24 by Newport News Shipbuilding and Dry Dock Company at Newport News, Virginia on 24 June 1899 and completed 19 October 1899 for the Morgan Line. The 4,604 GRT ship was assigned U.S. Official Number 136761, signal KPJW with home port of New York, New York. El Rio was intended for service between New York City and Gulf of Mexico seaports of New Orleans and Galveston, Texas. The Morgan Line was incorporated into the Southern Pacific system.

In March 2012, Mood Media announced the acquisition of DMX and its subsidiary AEI Music Network Inc. The deal was valued at $86.1 million. DMX, a US provider of multi-sensory branding services, uses music, video, and digital signage, scent, and audio/visual systems to create branded connections with customers that encourage loyalty. The company has provided its services to large-scale clients, including Gold’s Gym, McDonald’s, Burberry, and Whole Foods Market. In April 2014, Stingray Digital Group acquired the Canadian assets of DMX Music Inc. from Mood Media for $11.2 million.

Paul Gregory began his career in Hollywood as a personal assistant to clients such as Horace Heidt and Carmen Cavallaro. He became friends with actor Charles Laughton and organized a successful lecture tour for him across the United States from 1949 to 1950, earning $200,000. This money became the foundation for Gregory's later projects, including producing 17 Broadway plays in the 1950s and 1960s, such as *The Caine Mutiny Court-Martial*, *The Marriage Go-Around*, and *Lord Pengo*.

On September 22, 2020, Tiz the Law's owners announced he would skip the Preakness. In his absence, Kentucky Derby winner Authentic, who had also won the Haskell Stakes, was the favorite. Entries were taken on September 28, and Authentic drew post position 9 in the field of eleven for the October 3 race.

Following the death of Mohammed Toufic al-Assad in March 2015, control of his criminal network and its armed contingent passed to his son, Hussein Tawfiq al-Assad. Under Hussein’s leadership, the network was restructured and rebranded as the "Lions of Hussein Brigade" (Liwa Usud al-Hussein), officially founded in late June to early July 2015. According to Aymenn Jawad Al-Tamimi, the name of the group could refer both to its leader, Hussein Tawfiq al-Assad, and to Imam Hussein, a significant figure in Islamic history. The reorganization aimed to transform the group into a loyalist militia aligned with the Syrian government while maintaining its criminal activities on the side. By 2017, reports indicated that the Lions of Hussein had become part of the larger "Guardians of the Homeland" structure, though a commander of the group later denied any formal affiliation with the Syrian army. The brigade participated in several key offensives in 2015 and 2016, including battles in the Homs desert near Palmyra against the Islamic State, the Sahl al-Ghab campaign in late summer 2015, the Marj al-Sultan airbase offensive near Damascus towards the end of 2015, and operations in northern Latakia province. These actions underscored the group’s role as a volatile yet significant player in the Syrian conflict.

Larson was nominated for the 2006 Kim Yale Award for Best New Female Talent, and won the 2006 Ignatz Award in the category Promising New Talent. In 2007, Larson won the Eisner Award for Special Recognition (formerly known as "Talent Deserving of Wider Recognition"). She won the Eisner Award again in 2012 for her A Wrinkle in Time adaptation.

In the Abbasid Empire, many foreign works were translated into Arabic, large libraries were constructed, and scholars were welcomed. Under the caliphs Harun al-Rashid and his son Al-Ma'mun, the House of Wisdom in Baghdad flourished. Christian scholar Hunayn ibn Ishaq (809–873) was placed in charge of the translation work by the caliph. In his lifetime, Ishaq translated 116 writings, including works by Plato and Aristotle, into Syriac and Arabic.
Ofentse Mwase was born in Rustenburg, North West, South Africa. His filmmaking interest began in 2005. In 2009, he shot a Short film iGolide, and receive nomination for Best Cinematographer of 2010.

Relatively little is known about this mortar other than it being a muzzle-loading smoothbore mortar which was fired by a lanyard instead of being drop fired. The Type 93 may have been a transitional model and only produced in small numbers. It is described as being meant for motor transport due to its weight but it lacked a wheeled carriage for towing and was difficult to move.

The reservoir was first begun in 1878, as part of a system of reservoirs fed from the Sudbury River to supplement the Lake Cochituate system in Natick. Today's reservoir was created by excavation from 1894 to 1898, with construction undertaken in sections. It was begun by the City of Boston but completed by the newly formed Metropolitan Water Board (predecessor to the modern Massachusetts Water Resources Authority). All told, construction required moving about 4.5 million cubic yards (3.4 million cubic metres) of soil and boulders. Water began to fill the reservoir on February 8, 1897, with construction of the reservoir's new Sudbury Dam on the Stony Brook Branch of the Sudbury River completed later that year.

As of March 2007, Feldman was one of 13 Jewish baseball players in the major leagues, along with teammate Ian Kinsler. He was featured in the edition of Jewish Major Leaguers Baseball Cards, licensed by Major League Baseball, commemorating the Jewish major leaguers from 1871 through 2008. He joined, among others, Kinsler, Brad Ausmus, Kevin Youkilis, Ryan Braun, Gabe Kapler, Jason Marquis, John Grabow, Craig Breslow, Scott Schoeneweis and Jason Hirsh. In 2009, Feldman was named the Jewish Major Leaguers Pitcher of the Year, in a year in which he had more wins than any other Jewish pitcher since Steve Stone in 1980. Through 2017, he was seventh of all Jewish major leaguers in career strikeouts, behind Barry Latman, and seventh in career wins, behind Dave Roberts.

Sophie Alexander began her sporting career as a basketballer, playing for the Ballarat Rush in the South East Australian Basketball League for five years and starting her basketball journey with the Ballarat Pride in the Country Basketball League. In 2016, she transitioned to football, playing six matches for Redan Football Club in the Victorian Women's Football League before joining the Eastern Devils in 2017. As a midfielder and forward for the Devils, she showcased her ability to score from outside the 50-metre arc, a skill highlighted by coach Brendan Major. That year, she played 14 games and was invited to the AFLW draft combine but went undrafted. In 2018, Alexander joined Collingwood, where she was named in the VFLW Team of the Year and won the club’s VFLW Best and Fairest award, solidifying her reputation as a talented footballer.

The women's singles competition at the 2008 Summer Olympics saw significant changes in the tennis landscape following the retirement of Justine Henin, the incumbent world number one and reigning Olympic champion, on May 14, 2008. Henin, who cited exhaustion and the mental toll of the sport as reasons for her decision, left a void at the top of women's tennis. Injuries also plagued other top players, with Maria Sharapova, ranked world number three, and Ana Ivanovic, the 2008 French Open champion, struggling with form and fitness. Both Sharapova and Ivanovic were forced to withdraw from the Olympics due to injuries, with Sharapova sidelined by a shoulder issue and Ivanovic by a thumb injury. On August 11, 2008, Jelena Jankovic assumed the world number one ranking, becoming the first woman to achieve the feat without reaching a Grand Slam final. Among the contenders in Beijing were Venus and Serena Williams, who had won singles and doubles gold in Sydney 2000 and recently faced off in the Wimbledon final, as well as Russia’s Dinara Safina, a French Open finalist who had won back-to-back titles leading into the Games.

The Minnesota Twins selected Chargois as a pitcher in the second round, with the 72nd overall selection, of the 2012 MLB draft. He signed with the Twins, receiving a $712,600 signing bonus. He appeared in 12 games for the Elizabethton Twins of the Rookie-level Appalachian League, pitching to a 1.69 ERA, and recording five saves in 16 innings. He underwent Tommy John surgery in 2013. After missing the 2013 and 2014 season with recovery from surgery, he pitched for the Twins in an instructional league after the 2014 season.

Anand Lapsi testified that after dinner at the Hideaway Pizza on the night of July 22, 1999, he had gone to his new Mercedes-Benz in the parking lot, where a man wearing a blue bandana over the lower half of his face held a gun to his head and said, "give me the keys. Give me the keys." Lapsi positively identified Jones as the man who robbed him. Police later found the car in the parking lot of Jones' apartment with all of the owner's personal effects missing, and located the keys to the car in the Cutlass shared by Jones and Jordan.

Mutukula is located in the extreme southern part of Kyotera District, at the international border between Uganda and Tanzania. The town lies approximately 45 km by road south of Kyotera, where the district headquarters are situated, and about 81 km by road southwest of Masaka, the largest city in the sub-region. Mutukula is approximately 211 km by road southwest of Kampala, Uganda's capital. The town's coordinates are 1°00'00.0"S, 31°25'00.0"E, and it serves as a key land entry point for human and commercial traffic between Uganda and Tanzania.

Damian Martin joined the Perth Wildcats for the 2009–10 NBL season, following his former Sydney Spirit coach Rob Beveridge to the team. In his first season, Martin helped the Wildcats secure the NBL championship. His strong performances earned him a call-up to the Australian national team (Boomers) for the 2010 FIBA World Championship. In 2011, Martin won the NBL Best Defensive Player award for the first time and was named to the All-NBL First Team. Over the next three seasons, he won the Best Defensive Player award consecutively, becoming the third player in league history to achieve this feat. During the 2013 semi-final series, Martin suffered a partial Achilles tendon tear, ruling him out of the Grand Final. In January 2013, he was named co-captain of the Wildcats alongside Shawn Redhage, and later, for the 2013–14 season, he was named the sole captain of the team, solidifying his leadership role.

The Czech National Bank (CNB) is a leading example of a transparent central bank operating in an advanced economy. It has targeted inflation since 1998, adopting a 2% inflation target in 2010 with a tolerance band to ensure price stability. The CNB emphasizes transparency, publishing detailed forecasts and policy inputs. In a recent study of central bank transparency, which included numerous institutions, the CNB ranked among the top, reflecting its commitment to accountability and effective communication with the public.

J. T. Chargois was selected by the Minnesota Twins in the second round (72nd overall) of the 2012 MLB Draft out of Rice University, signing for a $712,600 bonus. He debuted with the Elizabethton Twins in the Rookie-level Appalachian League, recording a 1.69 ERA, five saves, and 16 innings pitched. After undergoing Tommy John surgery in 2013, Chargois missed the 2013 and 2014 seasons. He returned to pitch in the instructional league following the 2014 season, where his fastball reached 96-99 mph, occasionally touching 102 mph.

The area was first settled by Europeans around 1643 and became part of the town of Hempstead. During the American Revolution the southern part of Hempstead was primarily Tory, while the northern part, having been settled by Yankees, supported the revolution. Following the war, the Town of North Hempstead was split off in 1784.

Sally Kader, as President of the International Federation for Peace and Sustainable Development (IFPSD), has been a prominent advocate for children's rights and youth empowerment. She has promoted events like the Montessori Model United Nations Conferences and interfaith dialogue initiatives to foster leadership and understanding among youth. Kader has hosted panel discussions on human trafficking and supports the Virtual Global Task Force under the CIS, emphasizing international collaboration to combat exploitation. Her efforts highlight the importance of education and justice in empowering future generations.

Henry Y. Satterlee was born in 1843 in New York City to Jane Anna Yates Satterlee and her husband. His mother, Jane Anna, was the daughter of Henry Christopher Yates, a prominent figure in Albany, New York, who managed the Yates Mansion and held various political roles, including mayor of Albany. Henry Christopher Yates was the son of Joseph Christopher Yates, the first mayor of Schenectady and later governor of New York. Through his maternal lineage, Henry Y. Satterlee was also a descendant of Jellis Douwese Fonda, who emigrated to the Dutch colony of New Netherland in 1642, settling near Fort Orange.

On July 30, Oklahoma City police found Paul Howell's stolen GMC Suburban parked at a convenience store on the city's south side. To determine who left the vehicle there, officers canvassed the area and visited Kermit Lottie's nearby auto body shop, located four blocks away. Lottie, a convicted felon and police informant, told detectives that Ladell King and another individual had attempted to sell him the Suburban the day after Howell's murder. Recognizing the vehicle from news reports, Lottie refused to buy it. Lottie identified King as a middleman and provided information linking 19-year-old Julius Jones, known as "Day Day," to the crime.

Jairam Valjee Chouhan, a prominent railway contractor, earned recognition for his work with the Bengal Nagpur Railway, completing several bridges and doubling railway lines. Between 1921 and 1929, he camped near Paraghat for the construction of a railway bridge over the Arpa River and the doubling of the Bilaspur-Raigarh rail section. His main office was in Raigarh, where a colony named Jairam Valjee Colony honors his legacy. He frequently collaborated with fellow Mistri contractors, including Jagmal Gangji Sawaria from Bilaspur and Rai Saheb Harilal Shamji from Raigarh, on various railway and civil construction projects.

Prior to the seventh generation of video game consoles, video games were typically developed for a single console with only a few games receiving cross-platform releases across multiple consoles. This was due to the unique processing architecture of each console, making development for each a closed ecosystem and requiring additional effort to port to other systems. With the seventh generation of consoles, which saw console systems use similar processor hardware as personal computers, cross-platform development for both consoles and personal computers became much easier to achieve using standard software libraries, game engines, and scripting languages that isolate platform-specific details from the specific elements for the game itself. Such tools enabled games to be released simultaneously for multiple platforms.

Alan Wilson was made a deacon on 1 July 1979 by the Bishop of Oxford at Christ Church Cathedral, Oxford, and ordained a priest on 29 June 1980 by the same bishop at the same location. From 1979 to 1981, he served as Honorary Assistant Curate in Eynsham, continuing as Assistant Curate from 1981 to 1982. He met his future wife while studying at Oxford, where he also held an academic position funded by the university. Wilson’s early ministry laid the foundation for his later roles, including his appointment as Area Bishop of Buckingham in 2003.

Delayed-choice quantum eraser experiments raise profound questions about the nature of causality and the role of measurement in quantum mechanics. These experiments challenge classical intuitions by suggesting that outcomes at detector D0, where signal photons are observed, are determined by events at detectors D1, D2, D3, or D4, which measure the idler photons. This sequence appears to imply retrocausality, where future measurements influence past events. If the idler light paths were extended by a year, the detection of an idler photon at D1, D2, D3, or D4 would seemingly retroactively determine the behavior of the signal photon observed a year earlier. However, experiments have refuted the notion that knowledge of the idler photon's future fate allows for faster-than-light communication or true retrocausality. Instead, the observed phenomena are consistent with quantum mechanics, where interference patterns emerge only when idler photon information remains indeterminate.

Labour retained control, winning 41 seats, five of them gained from the Conservatives who dropped to 22 seats. Labour dominated in the east and south of the borough, retaining all their existing seats, while the Conservatives held most of their seats in the centre and west of the borough, though they lost two seats to Labour in Southgate Green ward, as well as single seats in Bush Hill Park, Chase and Winchmore Hill wards. For the second consecutive election, there was no representation from other parties on the council.

Amtrak provides passenger rail service in Fresno, California, through the historic Santa Fe Passenger Depot at 2650 Tulare Street. Future high-speed rail service is planned, with operations expected by 2030-2033.

The Pūrerehua can be made of bone, wood or stone, they are blade-like and swing on a long cord producing a loud, deep whirling that can be heard from a distance. A rapid spinning motion will start the music of the Purerehua'a song as it rotates and flutters. Uses vary from luring lizards, summoning rain, communicating and attracting a soul mate.

Numerous incidents of public disorder occurred from the start of the uprising. On January 28 the police withdrew from the streets of Egypt following break-ins to a number of prisons. Prison guards allegedly allowed and aided the escape of inmates. On February 2, a day that was dubbed the "Battle of the Camel", pro-government activists on camel- and horse-back stormed Tahrir Square in Downtown Cairo, where anti-regime demonstrators had set up camp, attacking them with Molotov cocktails, stones, chipped marble, swords and other weapons. This confrontation resulted in the deaths of 91 demonstrators and the injury of several hundreds. The government appointed a fact-finding panel to investigate the cause of these incidents, headed by judge Adel Koura.

VSHORADS or Very Short Range Air Defence System is a man portable air defence system (MANPAD) meant for neutralizing low altitude aerial threats at short ranges. The missile uses solid fuel based dual-thrust rocket motor and is developed by Research Centre Imarat . On 27 September 2022, DRDO conducted two successful launches from Integrated Test Range, Chandipur. To increase mid-air maneuverability, the missile is equiped with miniaturized Reaction Control System (RCS).

A balanced diet rich in protein, magnesium, and other nutrients may support nail health. Small studies suggest that 2.5 mg/day biotin supplementation improved brittle nails in 63-91% of participants (n=35-45) after 6-15 months, increasing nail firmness and thickness.

As of the 2000 census, Waupaca, Wisconsin had a population of 20,740, with 8,452 households and 5,814 families residing in the county. The population density was 24 people per square mile (9.3 people/km²). There were 11,187 housing units at an average density of 13 units per square mile (5.0 units/km²). The racial makeup of the county was 97.93% White, and 0.82% of the population were Hispanic or Latino of any race.

Bassam Tibi has criticized the left-green dominated German media for stifling debate about Islam, arguing it marginalizes liberal Muslims and empowers large Islamic organizations like the Muslim Brotherhood. He highlights Uwe Tellkamp, who faced backlash and was labeled a far-right sympathizer in mainstream media after criticizing Germany's migration policies. Tibi also condemns German authorities for failing to support liberal Muslims and for not challenging dominant Islamic organizations, which he believes undermines efforts to address conflicts between Islamic orthodoxy and democratic values.

Vibhuti, also known as holy ash or Bhasma, is a sacred substance in Hinduism, particularly in Shaivism, symbolizing purity, spiritual power, and detachment. It is prepared by burning the dung of native Indian cows with husk and fragrant herbs. Applied on the forehead, neck, chest, and other body parts, Vibhuti's three horizontal lines represent Lord Shiva's divine presence and the renunciation of material attachments. Combining these lines with Vishnu's 'U'-shaped tilaka signifies the unity of Shiva and Vishnu (Hari-Hara). Ash, as a symbol of transience, reminds devotees of the body's origin and return to dust, emphasizing the impermanence of material existence and the illusory nature of the world (maya), while encouraging spiritual devotion and purity.

Born in Bradford, Yorkshire, England, Swindells worked for a newspaper after leaving school aged 15. He served with the Royal Air Force and held various jobs before training as a Primary school teacher. While in training he wrote his first novel, When Darkness Comes, which was published by Brockhampton Press of Stenhousemuir in 1973. He combined writing with teaching until 1980 when he took up writing full-time. He was still writing as of his 71st birthday (20 March 2010).

The 16S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) gene is the most common housekeeping genetic marker for classifying and identifying bacterial species due to its presence in all bacteria and its unique structure of conserved and hypervariable regions. Approximately 1,500 base pairs in length, the 16S rRNA gene consists of highly conserved sequences essential for ribosomal function and hypervariable regions that enable discrimination between bacterial taxa. This dual structure allows for genus identification in nearly 100% of cases and species-level identification in 90–99% of cases. The use of 16S rRNA sequencing has revolutionized bacterial identification, especially for uncultivable or slow-growing species. Databases such as the RDP and INSDC provide comprehensive rRNA sequence information for bacteria, archaea, and eukarya, supporting accurate taxonomic classification and microbiome studies.

The duplex theory of sound localization, first proposed by Lord Rayleigh in the early 20th century, suggests that interaural time differences (ITDs) are primarily used to localize low-frequency sounds, typically below 1.5 kHz, while interaural level differences (ILDs) are crucial for localizing high-frequency sounds above this range. ITDs arise from the time delay between a sound reaching each ear, and they are processed in the Medial Superior Olive (MSO) of the auditory system, making them particularly effective for low-frequency localization. Conversely, ILDs, processed in the Lateral Superior Olive (LSO), result from differences in sound intensity between the ears and are more prominent at higher frequencies where the wavelength is comparable to or smaller than the diameter of the human head. Modern sound localization systems often compute the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) to isolate spectrotemporal components dominated by the desired sound source, enabling precise analysis of the signal's frequency and time characteristics. Systems with more than two microphones benefit from statistical methods, such as maximum likelihood estimation, to improve localization accuracy. Additionally, ITD-based systems, even without biomimetic instruments, can adapt to environmental variations, enhancing robustness. For listeners, maintaining a stable neural representation of slow temporal modulations (<4 Hz) ensures insensitivity to interaural phase spectrum details, even in noisy conditions.

Hart was born in Tampa, Florida, and graduated from Winter Park High School. He attended Seminole Junior College where he was catcher on the baseball team. In 1969 he won All-American honors and began his professional career as a catcher in the Montreal Expos organization. He caught with them for three seasons before leaving the organization and returning to Florida. He graduated in 1973 from the University of Central Florida with majors in history and physical education.

The Perth Heat were the first team to clinch a postseason position in the 2011–12 Australian Baseball League season, defeating the Brisbane Bandits 6-1 at Barbagallo Ballpark on 8 January. They secured a first-place finish on 19 January by defeating the Canberra Cavalry 10-5 at Narrabundah Ballpark. The Sydney Blue Sox finished a half-game ahead of a four-way tie involving the Melbourne Aces, Adelaide Bite, Brisbane Bandits, and Canberra Cavalry to claim the remaining spot in the major semi-final. After applying tiebreakers, the Melbourne Aces and Adelaide Bite finished third and fourth, respectively, securing their places in the postseason.

The shell of *Odostomia farallonensis* is small, ovate, and subconic, with a smooth, glossy surface and a white coloration. It typically measures about 2.5 mm in length. The protoconch consists of two whorls that are deeply immersed, with only the edge of the last whorl visible, giving a tilted appearance. The teleoconch comprises four whorls, which are slightly rounded and exhibit a well-defined suture. The summit of the shell is rounded and not sharply pointed. The aperture is oval in shape, with a thin outer lip. The columella is slender and features a single, weakly developed fold near its insertion, characteristic of the genus *Odostomia*.

Born in Milan, Barbieri started her career as the vocalist in a new wave group formed by alumni of the Santa Marta Music School in her hometown, first using her birth name Cristina, then adopting a number of stage names. In 1981, she was put under contract by Dischi Ricordi and adopted the stage name Diana Est. Shortly later, she made her record debut with "Tenax", an Italo disco song written by Enrico Ruggeri whose lyrics were partly in Italian and partly in Latin. In 1983, she got her main hit with the song "Le Louvre", still written by Ruggeri.

*Židovska meditacija – istraživanje mističnih staza judaizma*, published in 2003, provides an overview of Jewish mysticism, interpreting Kabbalistic concepts with accessible vocabulary for a broader audience.

Passenger rail service is provided by Amtrak San Joaquins. The main passenger rail station is the renovated historic Santa Fe Railroad Depot in Downtown Fresno. The city of Fresno is planned to be served by the future California High-Speed Rail.

Himesh Reshammiya composed all the songs featured in the film. The lyrics were penned by Sameer. According to the Indian trade website Box Office India, with around 13,00,000 units sold, this film's soundtrack album was the year's fourteenth highest-selling.

As of the 2000 census, there were 22,380 people, 8,642 households, and 6,222 families living in the county. The population density was 38 people per square mile (15/km2). There were 10,285 housing units at an average density of 18 per square mile (7/km2). The racial makeup of the county was 76.38% White (non-Hispanic), 22.24% Black or African American, 0.20% Native American, 0.22% Asian, 0.34% from other races, and 0.62% from two or more races. 3.82% of the population were Hispanic or Latino of any race.

Küssnacht has an area, (as of the 2004/09 survey) of 29.37 km2 (11.34 sq mi). Of this area, about 55.8% is used for agricultural purposes, while 26.4% is forested. Of the rest of the land, 16.5% is settled (buildings or roads) and 1.3% is unproductive land. In the 2004/09 survey a total of 270 ha (670 acres) or about 9.2% of the total area was covered with buildings, an increase of 82 ha (200 acres) over the 1982 amount. Over the same time period, the amount of recreational space in the municipality increased by 44 ha (110 acres) and is now about 2.07% of the total area.

El Rio, with hull number 1697, was launched by the Newport News Shipbuilding and Dry Dock Company in Newport News, Virginia, on May 26, 1910. Completed later that year for the Southern Pacific Steamship Company’s Morgan Line, the vessel had a gross register tonnage (GRT) of 5,000. Assigned U.S. Official Number 207018 and signal code KTRP, its home port was New York. El Rio was intended for service between New York and Gulf Coast seaports, part of the Southern Pacific Railroad system.

According to the duplex theory, ITDs have a greater contribution to the localisation of low frequency sounds (below 1 kHz), while ILDs are used in the localisation of high frequency sound. These approaches can be applied to selective reconstructions of spatialized signals, where spectrotemporal components believed to be dominated by the desired sound source are identified and isolated through the Short-time Fourier transform (STFT). Modern systems typically compute the STFT of the incoming signal from two or more microphones, and estimate the ITD or each spectrotemporal component by comparing the phases of the STFTs. An advantage to this approach is that it may be generalized to more than two microphones, which can improve accuracy in 3 dimensions and remove the front-back localization ambiguity that occurs with only two ears or microphones. Another advantage is that the ITD is relatively strong and easy to obtain without biomimetic instruments such as dummy heads and artificial pinnae, though these may still be used to enhance amplitude disparities. HRTF phase response is mostly linear and listeners are insensitive to the details of the interaural phase spectrum as long as the interaural time delay (ITD) of the combined low-frequency part of the waveform is maintained.

The Cavaliers debuted new uniforms before the start of the 2010–11 NBA season to coincide with the team returning to the original shades of wine and gold used from 1970 to 1983. The home uniform is white with a wine-and-gold horizontal stripe trim on the collar, sleeves, waistband and pant legs, the word "CAVALIERS" (in block style lettering) in wine on the front of the jersey with wine lettering for the name and number, and white shoes and socks. The road uniform is wine-colored with the same stripe trim, "Cleveland" in gold on the front of the jersey, gold lettering on the name and number, and black shoes and socks. An alternate jersey – gold with "CAVS" in wine on the front of the jersey, wine lettering on the name and number, white socks and shoes, and the same stripe trim as the other uniforms – was added for the 2012–13 season. All uniforms have the team motto – "All for one. One for all." – stitched on the inside of the collar and the secondary "Sword C" logo on the side of the pant legs. The logo used is the same piercing sword logo, updated to the classic wine-and-gold scheme.

Lyerla graduated early from high school and enrolled at Oregon in the spring of 2011 so he could take part in spring practices. During the team's spring game he had three catches for 37 yards while playing tight end. As a true freshman for the 2011 season, he played tight end and caught 7 passes for 147 yards and 5 touchdowns.

DeWeese was born January 31, 1934 in Rochester, Indiana. He began writing science fiction stories in grade school, beginning with a Mickey Mouse story he wrote. In high school, he wrote articles and science-fiction columns for his local newspaper. DeWeese became an active member of science fiction fandom, and his stories were published in science fiction fanzines such as Yandro, Fan-Fare, and The Chigger Patch of Fandom. He earned an associate degree in electronics from Valparaiso Technical Institute in 1953. He worked for General Motors' Delco Electronics Division as an electronics technician in Kokomo, Indiana, from 1954–1959, and as a technical writer (including for the Apollo Program) in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, from 1959 to 1974 (when he became a full-time freelance writer).

During his career, Maxwell also became known for the active promotion of xenophobic conspiracy theories. In his 1915 book Patriotism of Peace, Maxwell argued that the Colorado River needed comprehensive damming as otherwise, its water would be used by Japanese imperialists, who were colonizing Baja California. He referred to Southern and Eastern European immigrants as Huns and Vandals and preached that they should be excluded from joining Homecroft communities. Similarly, he promoted policies such as California’s Alien Land Laws, which prevented Asians from land ownership.

The introduction of federal discrimination protections for LGBTI people under the Gillard Government came with assurances to religious groups that they would retain the ability to discriminate against individuals deemed inconsistent with their beliefs, as outlined in sections 37 and 38 of the *Sex Discrimination Act 1984*. These sections allow religious bodies and educational institutions to discriminate based on sexual orientation, gender identity, marital or relationship status, or pregnancy if such actions align with their doctrines or are necessary to avoid injuring religious sensitivities. This legal framework enabled the dismissal of Craig Campbell, a Perth teacher, from South Coast Baptist College in 2017 after he revealed his sexuality on social media. Ahead of the 2016 election, the Greens pledged to remove these religious exemptions, while in January 2018, the Labor Party announced plans to review and limit such provisions. In response to the same-sex marriage debate, the Turnbull Government initiated a review of religious freedoms, which was later championed by Scott Morrison after he became Prime Minister, emphasizing the need to strengthen protections for religious groups while navigating broader societal shifts toward LGBTI equality.

Knowshon Moreno redshirted in 2006 and entered the 2007 season as a redshirt freshman under head coach Mark Richt. In the season opener against Oklahoma State, Moreno shared time as the starting running back with senior Thomas Brown, rushing for 70 yards on 12 carries and recording two receptions for 51 yards in a 35–14 victory. He scored his first career college touchdown in that game. In the following matchup against South Carolina, Moreno took on a larger role, rushing for 104 yards on 14 carries in a 16–12 loss.

Lewis continued working at Dykema Gossett after her nomination was withdrawn. She was named one of Michigan's top 10 lawyers of 2006 by Michigan Lawyers Weekly magazine. In an interview for that issue, Lewis told the magazine that service to her community was part of her essence. "Both of my parents were very active in the community as I was growing up, and from that I think I perceived that responsible grownups serve their communities in a voluntary capacity," she told the magazine. "It never occurred to me not to serve."

The pricing for the North American version of *Global Agenda* was announced alongside its release, reflecting a hybrid model of subscription-based and free-to-play mechanics. Described as a mix of MMO and shooter genres, its initial plan included a free base game with an optional subscription package called "Agency Elite" for added features.

The Sudbury Reservoir, located in Southborough, Massachusetts, was created between 1894 and 1898 with the construction of the Sudbury Dam on the Stony Brook branch of the Sudbury River. This reservoir was part of a larger system of reservoirs designed to supplement the Lake Cochituate system in Natick, which had been deemed insufficient for the growing water needs of the Boston metropolitan area. The project, overseen by the Boston Water Board, involved significant excavation, flooding approximately two square miles and nearly 10% of Southborough’s land area. Water began filling the reservoir in 1897, and it became a critical component of the region’s water supply system, later integrated into the Metropolitan District Commission’s watershed management efforts.

Satannish, an extra-dimensional Hell Lord created from the residual energy left by the destruction of Earth’s Elder Gods, is one of the most powerful demons in existence, rivaling even the might of Dormammu, his creator and occasional master. Often appearing to human sorcerers in a demonic form with green skin, large horns, and a grotesque face embedded in his torso, Satannish embodies concentrated malevolence. His vast mystical powers include near-limitless control over time, space, and matter, as well as the ability to cast devastating energy bolts, illusions, and demonic possessions. He is also a shapeshifter, teleporter, and telepath, with immense strength and durability. While his power is nearly invulnerable in his native dimension, it diminishes when he operates on Earth, though this limitation has lessened over time. Satannish’s power grows with the worship of his followers, and he has demonstrated his destructive capabilities by annihilating the Skrulls in Avalon after granting Pete Wisdom’s wish to rid Great Britain of their presence. Despite his immense power, Satannish remains subordinate to Dormammu, acting as an agent of the Dread Faltine when required.

Manfred Feist suffered a stroke in 2014, which ultimately led to his death in Santiago, Chile, on April 21, 2014. His passing was announced several days later in *Bildende Kunst*. Speculation arose about whether Margot Honecker, residing in Chile since 1992, might return to Germany for his funeral.

The *hala*, a demon whose name derives from the Greek word for "hail," was a prominent figure in Slavic folklore, particularly in Bulgarian traditions. In Southwestern Bulgaria, the hala was believed to cause strong winds and whirlwinds, while in Western Bulgaria, the *halla* was regarded as a dragon-like creature. It was said to guard and contain treasures, often associated with storms and chaos. In Western Bulgarian folklore, the halla was considered alongside the *smok*, a creature roughly equated with a dragon but actually identified as a serpent. These beings were central to explaining destructive natural phenomena, such as violent storms, in the region's mythology.

In November 1971, Thaler was elected to the New York Supreme Court. On December 21, he and three associates were indicted for selling $800,000 worth of stolen Treasury bills in 1970. On December 23, 1971, he pleaded not guilty. In February 1972, four counts of perjury were added to the indictment. On March 23, he was convicted of unlawfully receiving and disposing of stolen securities, and of perjury. On August 8, he was sentenced to one year in prison, and fined $10,000. On November 2, he was disbarred by the Appellate Division. On March 5, 1973, the conviction was upheld by the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit.

Bellah was elected a Fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1967. In 1996, he was elected to the American Philosophical Society. He received the National Humanities Medal in 2000 from President Bill Clinton, in part for "his efforts to illuminate the importance of community in American society." In 2007, he received the American Academy of Religion Martin E. Marty Award for the Public Understanding of Religion. In 2008, he received the honorary doctorate of the Max Weber Centre of the University of Erfurt.

The Pascoe Vale area was part of the original John Pascoe Fawkner property "Pascoeville". In 1841, Dr Farquhar McCrae, a wealthy surgeon, purchased land called 'La Rose' in what is now known as Pascoe Vale South. The house he built in 1842 or 1843 is now known as Wentworth House, and is the oldest known private dwelling in Victoria still standing on its original site, and the fifth oldest building in Victoria. It is located at 22 Le Cateau Street, Pascoe Vale South.

The Star (stylised as THE STAR) is one of the brands that First Portsmouth, Fareham & Gosport use which follows along the A3 that replaced the Portsdown and Horndean tram service which had been superseded by motor buses in 1935. The brand itself was started in 2008 with the creation of the A3 corridor. There are two routes under this brand: Route 7 runs along London road (A2407) from Portsmouth university via Cosham and Crookhorn before ending in Wecock Farm whilst route 8 uses the A3 starting its journey at Old Portsmouth Clarence Pier via The hard, Cosham, Purbrook and Horndean and terminating at Catherington.

In December 2009, the Diocese of Los Angeles elected Mary Douglas Glasspool, a lesbian priest with a female partner as a suffragan bishop. She was consecrated on 15 May 2010. Leaders from 20 Anglican provinces meeting in Singapore in April 2010 declared that the election and intended consecration of Glasspool "demonstrated, yet again, a total disregard for the mind of the Communion".

Gregory went to Hollywood where he worked as a personal assistant for clients like Horace Heidt and Carmen Cavallaro. He became friends with actor Charles Laughton and organized a successful lecture tour which Laughton made through the United States between 1949 and 1950. They earned $200,000 during this reading tour; the money worked as the basis for other projects. Gregory afterwards produced 17 Broadway plays during the 1950s and 1960s, among them The Caine Mutiny Court-Martial, 3 for Tonight, The Marriage Go-Around and Lord Pengo.

In 2019 Bakov published a book called "The state is You!" where disclosed in details how his talks with the abovementioned governments went on. He reported that he initially was always met with enthusiasm, the officials gladly took money from him and promised support but later were all giving up. He suggests there was some hidden resist against him on unknown grounds. According to book, the 7-years Gambia campaign included plain fraud towards him in the course of regime change and concurrent chaos in one of the poorest Africa's nations while initially he was allowed by Yahya Jammeh to make measurements of oceanic bottom for future artificial islands and the whole engineering draft was completed (headed by his sons who have engineering education). He emphasized that many people who talked to him about devotion to Christian Monarchy and God were actually interested only in money.
